question: Refer to the first-order model for auction price $(y)$ considered in Example 12.1. Interpret the estimates of the $ eta$ parameters in the model.
answer:
The least squares prediction equation, as given in Example 12.1, is $\hat{y}=-1,339+ 12.74 x_1+85.95 x_2$. We know that with first-order models, $ eta_1$ represents the slope of the line relating $y$ to $x_1$ for fixed $x_2$. That is, $ eta_1$ measures the change in $E(y)$ for every 1-unit increase in $x_1$ when the other independent variable in the model is held fixed. A similar
statement can be made about $ eta_2:  eta_2$ measures the change in $E(y)$ for every 1-unit increase in $x_2$ when the other $x$ in the model is held fixed. Consequently, we obtain the following interpretations:
$\hat{ eta}_1=12.74$ : We estimate the mean auction price $E(y)$ of an antique clock to increase $\$ 12.74$ for every 1-year increase in age ( $x_1$ ) when the number of bidders ( $x_2$ ) is held fixed.
$\hat{ eta}_2=85.95$ : We estimate the mean auction price $E(y)$ of an antique clock to increase $\$ 85.95$ for every 1-bidder increase in the number of bidders ( $x_2$ ) when age $\left(x_1ight)$ is held fixed.
The value $\hat{ eta}_0=-1,339$ does not have a meaningful interpretation in this example. To see this, note that $\hat{y}=\hat{ eta}_0$ when $x_1=x_2=0$. Thus, $\hat{ eta}_0=-1,339$ represents the estimated mean auction price when the values of all the independent variables are set equal to 0 . Because an antique clock with these characteristics - an age of 0 years and 0 bidders on the clock-is not practical, the value of $\hat{ eta}_0$ has no meaningful interpretation.

meta_info.caption: Interpreting the $ eta$ Estimates: Clock Auction Price Model
meta_info.section: 12.2 Estimating and Making Inferences about the $\boldsymbol{\beta}$ Parameters
